persona.name: Alex Rivera
persona.focus: System design, algorithms, architecture, problem-solving, technical depth

questions.question: Let's start with something straightforward — design a REST API for a todo list app. Walk me through the endpoints, data model, and architecture.
questions.expected_answer: Endpoints: GET /todos (list), POST /todos (create), GET /todos/:id (read), PUT /todos/:id (update), DELETE /todos/:id (delete). Data model: id, title, description, completed (boolean), created_at, updated_at. Architecture: web server → API handler → database. Use proper HTTP status codes (200, 201, 204, 400, 404). Input validation on create/update. Pagination for list endpoint. Consider: authentication (who owns each todo), error response format, filtering/sorting.
follow_ups: How would you add user authentication?
How would you handle pagination?
What database would you choose and why?

questions.question: Tell me about SQL versus NoSQL databases — when would you reach for one over the other?
questions.expected_answer: SQL databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL): structured data, tables with relationships, ACID transactions, SQL query language, schema enforcement. NoSQL (MongoDB, DynamoDB, Redis): flexible schema, horizontal scaling, various data models (document, key-value, graph, column-family). Choose SQL when: data is relational, need ACID transactions, complex queries with joins. Choose NoSQL when: schema varies, need horizontal scale, simple access patterns, high throughput with eventual consistency. Most applications start with SQL. Consider: PostgreSQL can handle JSON documents too, bridging the gap.
follow_ups: What is ACID and why does it matter?
What is an index and how does it speed up queries?
When would you use Redis?

questions.question: Classic one — design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Walk me through the core architecture, data model, and how you'd think about scaling it.
questions.expected_answer: Core: convert long URLs to short codes, redirect on access. Generation: base62 encoding of auto-increment ID or random string with collision check. Data model: short_code (indexed), original_url, created_at, user_id, click_count. Architecture: load balancer → web servers → cache (Redis for hot URLs) → database. Read-heavy workload: cache aggressively, CDN for redirects. Scaling: read replicas for database, consistent hashing for cache, analytics via async event stream (Kafka). Edge cases: custom URLs, expiration, abuse prevention (rate limiting, blocklist), analytics (clicks by geography/time).
follow_ups: How would you handle billions of URLs?
How do you ensure short codes are unique in a distributed system?
How would you implement real-time click analytics?

questions.question: So you're building caching for an e-commerce product catalog. What's your strategy, and what are the main things you'd need to think about?
questions.expected_answer: Multi-layer caching: CDN for product images and static assets, application cache (Redis) for product data, database query cache. Cache strategy: cache-aside (read from cache, on miss read from DB and populate cache). Invalidation: TTL-based for simplicity, event-driven invalidation (product updated → invalidate cache entry) for freshness. Key design: cache key schema (product:id, category:slug:page), cache size limits with LRU eviction, cache warming for popular products. Considerations: cache stampede prevention (distributed locks or probabilistic early expiration), eventual consistency (stale data acceptable for catalog, not for pricing/inventory), cache serialization format (MessagePack > JSON for performance).
follow_ups: How do you prevent cache stampede?
What's the difference between cache-aside and write-through?
How do you handle cache invalidation across multiple servers?

questions.question: Design a notification system that can push millions of messages — we're talking push notifications, emails, SMS, the whole thing. How would you architect this?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: API receives notification request → message queue (Kafka/SQS) for decoupling → worker pools per channel (email, push, SMS). Each channel has its own queue and workers to handle different throughput/latency requirements. Data model: notification template, user preferences (channels, frequency), delivery status tracking. Key components: preference service (user opted-in channels), rate limiting (per user, per channel), deduplication (idempotency key), retry with exponential backoff, priority levels (urgent vs batch). Scaling: partition queues by priority, horizontal worker scaling, batch processing for non-urgent notifications. Monitoring: delivery rate, bounce rate, latency per channel.
follow_ups: How do you handle user notification preferences at scale?
How do you ensure exactly-once delivery?
How would you implement notification batching/digesting?

questions.question: Let's design a real-time chat system like Slack — channels, direct messages, file sharing, the whole thing. We're talking 100K+ employees. How would you build this?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: WebSocket connection layer (stateful, needs sticky sessions or connection registry), message routing service, persistence layer. Connection management: WebSocket servers with connection registry (Redis) mapping user → server. Message flow: client → WS server → message service → persist + fan-out to recipients' WS servers. Data model: channels, messages (partitioned by channel), membership, read positions. Scaling: shard channels across message service instances, separate read/write paths, hot channels get dedicated resources. File sharing: upload to object storage (S3), generate thumbnails asynchronously, store metadata in message DB. Search: index messages in Elasticsearch. Presence: heartbeat-based online status with distributed presence service. Delivery guarantees: message IDs for deduplication, client-side acknowledgment, offline queue for mobile push.
follow_ups: How do you handle message ordering guarantees?
How would you implement @mentions and notifications?
How do you handle the 'thundering herd' problem when a company-wide channel gets a message?

questions.question: You need to build rate limiting across multiple service instances and data centers. Walk me through how you'd design that — what are the tricky parts?
questions.expected_answer: Algorithms: token bucket (smooth traffic), sliding window (precise counting), leaky bucket (constant output rate). Distributed: centralized counter in Redis with Lua scripts for atomicity (INCR + EXPIRE). Multi-datacenter: either global Redis with latency trade-off, or local rate limiters with eventual synchronization (accept slight over-limit). Implementation: token bucket in Redis — key per (user, endpoint), Lua script atomically checks and decrements tokens, TTL for automatic refill. Fallback: local in-memory rate limiter when Redis is unavailable (degrade gracefully, over-allow rather than block). Configuration: rate limits as configuration (not code), per-tier limits (free vs paid), per-endpoint limits. Monitoring: track rate limit hits, false positives, Redis latency. Edge cases: distributed clock skew, burst handling, graceful degradation.
follow_ups: How do you handle rate limiting across multiple data centers?
What happens when your Redis rate limiter goes down?
How do you test rate limiting behavior?

questions.question: Design the architecture for a multi-tenant SaaS platform — you need to support everything from tiny tenants with 10 users to enterprise ones with 100K. How do you handle the data isolation and everything else?
questions.expected_answer: Tenancy models: shared database with tenant_id column (cheapest, least isolation), schema-per-tenant (moderate isolation), database-per-tenant (strongest isolation, most expensive). Decision: tier-based — small tenants share infrastructure, enterprise tenants get dedicated resources. Architecture: API gateway with tenant resolution (subdomain or header), tenant-aware middleware injects tenant context, query filters enforce data isolation. Scaling: control plane (tenant management, billing, provisioning) separate from data plane (application workloads). Noisy neighbor prevention: resource quotas per tenant, separate compute for large tenants, queue partitioning. Data migration: tools for moving tenants between tiers as they grow. Compliance: data residency (geo-aware routing), tenant data export/deletion, audit logging. Key challenge: the 'cell architecture' — group tenants into cells (independent stacks), route at the edge, scale cells independently. This limits blast radius and enables independent deployment.
follow_ups: How do you handle schema migrations across hundreds of tenant databases?
What's your approach to tenant-level monitoring and alerting?
How do you handle cross-tenant features (like shared marketplaces)?

questions.question: Here's a real one — you need to handle 10 million financial transactions a day, with exactly-once semantics and a complete audit trail. How do you design that system?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: event-driven with immutable event log. Ingestion: API gateway → validation service → Kafka (partitioned by account, ordered within partition). Processing: Kafka consumer groups with manual offset management for exactly-once. Pattern: idempotent processing with transaction IDs, transactional outbox for state changes + events atomically. State: event sourcing — store events, derive current state. Database: PostgreSQL with partitioned tables (by date), write-ahead for durability. Reconciliation: batch job compares expected vs actual state, flags discrepancies. Auditability: immutable event log, every state change traceable to a source event, regulatory-compliant retention (7+ years). Disaster recovery: multi-AZ replication, RPO < 1 second. Monitoring: real-time alerting on anomalies (unusual transaction volumes, processing latency spikes). Security: encryption at rest and in transit, field-level encryption for PII, access logging.
follow_ups: How do you handle reconciliation when events are processed out of order?
What's your approach to data retention and archival for compliance?
How do you ensure the system continues operating during a partial outage?

questions.question: Let's talk about a responsive navigation component — something that works great on both mobile and desktop. Walk me through how you'd approach that.
questions.expected_answer: Desktop: horizontal nav bar with links, dropdowns for sub-navigation. Mobile: hamburger menu icon that opens a sidebar/drawer. Implementation: CSS media queries for breakpoint-based layout changes, or container queries for component-level responsiveness. Use semantic HTML (nav, ul, li, a). Accessibility: keyboard navigation, ARIA labels for hamburger button, focus management when drawer opens/closes, escape to close. Performance: use CSS transitions for animations (not JS), lazy-load dropdown content. Consider: touch targets (44x44px minimum for mobile), progressive enhancement, skip-to-content link.
follow_ups: How would you handle accessibility for the mobile menu?
How do you decide breakpoint values?
How would you test this across different devices?

questions.question: Picture a data-heavy dashboard — lots of real-time updates, complex filtering options, interactive charts. How would you architect something like that on the frontend?
questions.expected_answer: Data layer: React Query/TanStack Query for server state (polling or WebSocket for real-time), normalized cache to avoid duplicate data. State management: URL params for filter state (shareable, bookmarkable), local state for UI interactions. Component architecture: container/presenter pattern — data fetching in containers, pure rendering in presenters. Charts: Recharts or D3 for visualization, virtualized rendering for large datasets. Performance: memoize expensive calculations (useMemo), virtualize long lists (react-window), debounce filter inputs, code-split chart libraries, skeleton loading states. Real-time: WebSocket connection with reconnection logic, optimistic UI updates, conflict resolution for concurrent edits. Responsive: condensed chart views on mobile, collapsible filter panels.
follow_ups: How do you handle WebSocket reconnection and state synchronization?
How would you optimize rendering performance with thousands of data points?
How do you handle cross-filter interactions?

questions.question: You're building a design system and component library for a company with multiple product teams. How do you balance consistency with giving teams the flexibility they need?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: core design tokens (colors, spacing, typography) as CSS custom properties or JS constants, primitive components (Button, Input, Card) with flexible APIs, composite components (DataTable, Form) built from primitives. Distribution: published as NPM package(s), versioned with semantic versioning, changesets for changelogs. Documentation: Storybook with interactive examples, usage guidelines, accessibility notes, do/don't examples. Flexibility: composition over configuration, render props or slots for customization, theme variants for product-specific branding. Governance: RFC process for new components, design review with design team, deprecation policy with migration guides. Testing: visual regression with Chromatic, unit tests for behavior, accessibility audits (axe). Adoption: track usage metrics, measure consistency scores, provide codemods for breaking changes.
follow_ups: How do you handle breaking changes without disrupting product teams?
How do you ensure accessibility across all components?
How do you decide what belongs in the design system vs. a product-specific component?

questions.question: You're designing the frontend for a platform with multiple products that need independent deployment, shared auth, the whole micro-frontend thing. How do you structure that?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture options: Module Federation (webpack), import maps with native ESM, or single-spa as runtime orchestrator. Decision depends on team autonomy needs. Shell application: handles routing, authentication, shared layout (header/sidebar), and loading micro-frontends. Shared dependencies: React, design system loaded once by shell, shared via Module Federation. Communication: custom events for cross-MFE communication, shared state via URL params or event bus (minimal). Deployment: each MFE has independent CI/CD, deploys to CDN, shell discovers latest version via manifest. Challenges: consistent styling (shared design tokens), performance (avoid bundle duplication), testing (contract tests between shell and MFEs), SEO (SSR at shell level). Progressive approach: start monolith, extract MFEs only when team boundaries or deployment independence demand it.
follow_ups: How do you handle shared state (like user authentication) across micro-frontends?
What's the testing strategy for a micro-frontend architecture?
How do you prevent the architecture from becoming more complex than the problems it solves?

questions.question: Let's talk about ML pipelines — walk me through how you'd set up training for a text classification model, from raw data all the way to a trained model.
questions.expected_answer: Steps: 1) Data collection — gather labeled text data, store in structured format (CSV/Parquet). 2) Data preprocessing — clean text (lowercase, remove punctuation, handle special chars), tokenization, split into train/validation/test sets (80/10/10). 3) Feature extraction — TF-IDF for simple models, or tokenizer + embeddings for deep learning (BERT, sentence-transformers). 4) Model selection — start simple (logistic regression, random forest on TF-IDF), then try fine-tuned transformer if needed. 5) Training — use scikit-learn or PyTorch/HuggingFace Transformers, track metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1). 6) Evaluation — confusion matrix, per-class metrics, evaluate on held-out test set. 7) Versioning — save model artifacts with metadata (hyperparameters, training data hash, metrics). Tools: Jupyter for exploration, then move to scripts. Consider: class imbalance (oversampling, class weights), reproducibility (random seeds, pinned dependencies).
follow_ups: How would you handle class imbalance in your dataset?
What metrics would you use to evaluate the model and why?
How do you decide between a simple model and a deep learning approach?

questions.question: Tell me about feature stores — why would ML teams use one, and what problems are you actually solving there?
questions.expected_answer: A feature store is a centralized repository for storing, managing, and serving ML features. Problems solved: 1) Training-serving skew — ensures the same feature computation logic is used in training and inference. 2) Feature reuse — teams share features instead of recomputing the same transformations. 3) Point-in-time correctness — for training data, you need features as they were at the time of each example, not the current value (avoids data leakage). 4) Online/offline serving — batch features for training (offline store, e.g., Parquet in S3) and low-latency features for inference (online store, e.g., Redis/DynamoDB). Examples: Feast (open source), Tecton, Amazon SageMaker Feature Store. A feature store typically has: feature definitions (schema + transformation logic), materialization jobs (compute and store features), and a serving API (get features by entity key). Consider: who owns features, versioning, data quality monitoring.
follow_ups: What's the difference between an online and offline feature store?
What is training-serving skew and why is it dangerous?
How would you organize features across multiple ML teams?

questions.question: Let's design an end-to-end ML system for recommendations. You're handling data ingestion, training, and serving predictions in real time to users. Walk me through it.
questions.expected_answer: Data: user interaction events (clicks, purchases, ratings) collected via event stream (Kafka), user and item metadata from application DB. Feature engineering: user features (purchase history, browsing patterns, demographics), item features (category, price, popularity), interaction features (recency, frequency). Offline pipeline: Airflow-orchestrated — extract events → compute features (Spark) → store in feature store → train model (collaborative filtering like ALS, or neural like two-tower model) → evaluate (precision@k, recall@k, NDCG) → register in model registry (MLflow). Online serving: user request → fetch user + candidate item features from feature store → model inference (candidate generation → ranking) → apply business rules (diversity, freshness, already-seen filter) → return top-N. Architecture: candidate generation (fast, approximate — ANN index like FAISS) → ranking model (precise, score top-100 candidates). Training cadence: daily retrain with latest interactions, weekly full retrain. Monitoring: recommendation quality metrics, click-through rate, coverage, novelty.
follow_ups: How do you handle the cold-start problem for new users or new items?
How would you set up A/B testing for different recommendation models?
How do you balance relevance vs diversity in recommendations?

questions.question: You need to train and evaluate tons of ML models in parallel, track experiments, keep everything reproducible. How would you design that system?
questions.expected_answer: Experiment tracking: MLflow or Weights & Biases — log hyperparameters, metrics, artifacts, code version for every run. Reproducibility: pin all dependencies (requirements.txt or conda env), set random seeds, version training data (DVC or Delta Lake), store git commit hash with each run. Parallel training: orchestrate with Airflow or Kubernetes Jobs — each experiment is a container with defined resources (GPU type/count, memory). Hyperparameter tuning: Optuna or Ray Tune for automated search (Bayesian optimization, early stopping of bad trials). Evaluation: standardized evaluation suite — same test set, same metrics, statistical significance tests (bootstrap CI) before declaring a winner. Model registry: MLflow Model Registry — staging → production promotion flow with approval. Compute: spot/preemptible instances for training (with checkpointing for interruption), dedicated instances for serving. Storage: model artifacts in S3/GCS, feature data in feature store, experiment metadata in tracking server.
follow_ups: How do you version your training data alongside model versions?
How do you decide when a new model is good enough to deploy?
What's your strategy for managing GPU resources across teams?

questions.question: Design a real-time ML serving platform that can handle 10,000+ requests per second. You're doing feature engineering, inference, A/B testing — all at scale. Walk me through your architecture.
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: online feature store (Redis/DynamoDB for pre-computed features, Flink for real-time feature computation), model serving (TensorFlow Serving, Triton, or custom gRPC service), experiment platform for A/B testing. Feature pipeline: batch features computed daily (Spark → feature store), real-time features from event stream (Kafka → Flink → feature store). Serving: feature retrieval → model inference → response. Low latency: pre-warm model containers, batch inference requests (dynamic batching in Triton), cache popular predictions, quantize models (FP16/INT8). A/B testing: experiment assignment at request time (hash-based bucketing for consistency), metrics collection via event stream, statistical analysis pipeline. Model lifecycle: shadow mode (run new model in parallel without serving), canary deployment (gradual traffic shift), automatic rollback on metric degradation. Monitoring: prediction distribution drift, feature drift, latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99), model accuracy decay. Infrastructure: autoscaling GPU pods in K8s, model warm-up on deployment, graceful draining on shutdown.
follow_ups: How do you detect and handle model drift in production?
What's your approach to feature store design for both batch and real-time?
How do you ensure reproducibility of training vs serving features?

questions.question: You have dozens of ML models in production and need to monitor them closely — catching data drift, model degradation, pipeline failures. How do you build that system?
questions.expected_answer: Data drift detection: monitor input feature distributions (KL divergence, PSI, KS test) comparing production data to training data, alert when drift exceeds threshold. Prediction drift: monitor output distribution shifts even without ground truth labels. Model performance: when labels are available (delayed ground truth), compute accuracy/AUC trends, detect degradation with statistical process control. Pipeline monitoring: Airflow/Prefect health checks, data freshness (feature store staleness), training pipeline success/failure, serving latency and error rates. Architecture: production inference logs → event stream (Kafka) → drift computation service (batch + streaming) → metrics store (Prometheus/InfluxDB) → alerting (PagerDuty) + dashboards (Grafana). Per-model dashboards: feature distributions, prediction distributions, latency, throughput, error rate, data quality. Automated remediation: trigger model retrain when drift exceeds threshold, rollback to previous model if performance drops, circuit-breaker for serving failures (fallback to rule-based). Governance: model cards documenting expected data distributions, SLOs per model (latency, accuracy), regular model review cadence.
follow_ups: How do you handle monitoring when ground truth labels are delayed by weeks?
What's the difference between data drift and concept drift?
How do you prioritize alerts across 50+ models in production?

questions.question: You're designing an ML platform for a big company with 20+ ML teams. You need to cover the full lifecycle from experimentation to production. How do you standardize without slowing down the teams?
questions.expected_answer: Platform layers: 1) Data layer — centralized feature store with self-service feature registration, data catalog (Amundsen/DataHub) for discoverability, data quality framework. 2) Training layer — managed training infrastructure (Kubeflow/SageMaker), notebook environments with GPU access, experiment tracking (MLflow), hyperparameter tuning service. 3) Serving layer — unified model serving (Seldon/KServe on K8s) with autoscaling, A/B testing framework, shadow deployment capability. 4) Monitoring layer — standardized model monitoring (drift detection, performance tracking), alerting, automated retraining triggers. Self-service: teams define models via declarative configs (model.yaml with serving requirements, feature dependencies, SLOs), platform handles infrastructure. Standardization: golden paths (opinionated templates) for common patterns (batch prediction, real-time serving, LLM fine-tuning), but escape hatches for custom needs. Governance: model registry with approval workflow, model cards, lineage tracking (data → features → model → predictions), cost attribution per team. Organizational: platform team owns shared infrastructure, ML teams own models and features, SRE owns production reliability. Key metrics: time from experiment to production, platform adoption rate, infrastructure cost per prediction.
follow_ups: How do you handle the tension between standardization and team autonomy?
What's your approach to cost management when teams have unlimited GPU access?
How do you design the platform to support both traditional ML and LLM workloads?

questions.question: Build a massive A/B testing platform for ML models that can handle thousands of concurrent experiments across multiple systems. How do you maintain statistical rigor at that scale?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: experiment assignment service (hash-based user bucketing for consistency, supports mutual exclusion and layered experiments), metrics collection pipeline (event stream → aggregation → statistical analysis), experiment management UI. Assignment: deterministic hashing (user_id + experiment_id → bucket), layered experiments (Google's overlapping experiment infrastructure — each layer is independent), mutual exclusion groups for conflicting experiments. Statistical engine: sequential testing (always-valid p-values) for faster decisions, CUPED variance reduction, multiple comparison correction (Bonferroni or FDR), Bayesian approach for business-friendly probability statements. Metrics: guardrail metrics (must not degrade), primary metrics (optimization target), secondary metrics (observational). Pipeline: raw events → Kafka → Flink (real-time metric aggregation) → daily batch computation → statistical tests → dashboard. Scale considerations: pre-compute metric aggregates per experiment-variant, approximate algorithms for high-cardinality metrics, sample ratio mismatch detection (data quality). Governance: experiment review before launch (sample size calculation, metric selection), automated experiment duration recommendation, mandatory guardrail metrics, post-experiment reports stored for institutional learning.
follow_ups: How do you handle interactions between overlapping experiments?
What's your approach to long-running experiments where user behavior changes over time?
How do you ensure experiment results are trustworthy when dealing with network effects?

questions.question: Let's build a simple blog app — you need user authentication, frontend, backend, the whole thing. Walk me through how you'd approach it.
questions.expected_answer: Backend: REST API with endpoints for auth (register, login, logout), posts (CRUD), and comments. Use JWT or session-based auth. Database: PostgreSQL with users, posts, comments tables. Relationships: user has many posts, post has many comments. Frontend: React/Next.js app with pages for login, post list, single post, create/edit post. State management: store auth token in httpOnly cookie (secure) or localStorage (simpler). API calls: fetch/axios with auth header. Component structure: Layout → pages → components (PostCard, CommentList, AuthForm). Styling: Tailwind CSS or CSS modules. Key considerations: input validation on both frontend and backend, XSS prevention (sanitize HTML in posts), CSRF protection, password hashing (bcrypt), proper error handling and loading states.
follow_ups: How would you handle image uploads for blog posts?
How do you decide what validation to put on the frontend vs backend?
How would you implement pagination for the post list?

questions.question: Take me through the request lifecycle in a full-stack app — what happens from the moment someone clicks a button to when they see the updated data?
questions.expected_answer: 1) User clicks button → React event handler fires. 2) Frontend: set loading state, make HTTP request (fetch/axios) to API endpoint. 3) Network: DNS resolution, TCP connection (reused if keep-alive), TLS handshake if HTTPS, send HTTP request with headers (auth token, content-type). 4) Backend: web server receives request → middleware (auth check, CORS, rate limiting) → route handler → validate input → service/business logic → database query (ORM or raw SQL) → format response → send HTTP response. 5) Network: response travels back. 6) Frontend: receive response → update state → React re-renders affected components → user sees updated UI. Error path: network failure → catch error → show error toast/message → optionally retry. Optimistic updates: update UI immediately, roll back on error. Caching: browser cache, CDN, application cache (Redis), database query cache.
follow_ups: What's the difference between optimistic and pessimistic UI updates?
How does CORS work and why is it important?
Where would you add caching in this lifecycle?

questions.question: Design a real-time collaborative task app like Trello — multiple people editing at the same time. Walk me through the full-stack architecture including how you'd handle real-time updates.
questions.expected_answer: Backend: Node.js/Python API server + WebSocket server (Socket.io or native WS). Database: PostgreSQL for persistent data (boards, lists, cards, users, memberships), Redis for real-time state and pub/sub. API: REST for CRUD operations, WebSocket for real-time events (card moved, comment added, user typing). Data model: boards → lists → cards (ordered by position field), card has assignees, labels, comments, attachments. Real-time: when user moves a card, broadcast position update to all board members via WebSocket. Conflict resolution: last-write-wins for most fields, operational transform for simultaneous card moves. Frontend: React with optimistic updates — move card immediately in UI, send update to server, roll back on error. State: local state synced via WebSocket events, React Query for server state. Drag-and-drop: react-beautiful-dnd or dnd-kit. Auth: JWT with refresh tokens, board-level permissions (owner, member, viewer).
follow_ups: How do you handle conflicts when two users move the same card simultaneously?
How would you implement undo/redo for card operations?
How do you optimize WebSocket connections for users with many boards?

questions.question: Let's design a file upload system that handles big files — up to 1GB. You need progress tracking, background processing, the whole thing. How do you build it?
questions.expected_answer: Upload: chunked upload from frontend (split file into 5MB chunks, upload sequentially or in parallel with concurrency limit). Backend: receive chunks, store temporarily, reassemble on completion. Progress: track chunks received / total chunks, send progress via SSE or WebSocket. Storage: upload to object storage (S3) using presigned URLs (bypass server for large files) or multipart upload API. Processing: after upload complete, push job to message queue (SQS/Redis), background worker processes file (resize images, transcode video, parse CSV). Status: poll or WebSocket for processing status updates. Frontend: drag-and-drop zone, progress bar per file, cancel/retry support, file type validation before upload. Error handling: resume interrupted uploads (track uploaded chunks), retry failed chunks, timeout handling. Security: file type validation (server-side, not just extension), virus scanning, size limits, signed URLs with expiration.
follow_ups: How would you implement resumable uploads after a network failure?
How do you handle virus scanning for uploaded files?
What's your approach to generating thumbnails for different file types?

questions.question: Design a multi-tenant SaaS dashboard — role-based access, real-time data, white-labeling support. Walk me through how you'd architect the whole thing.
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: Next.js frontend (SSR for SEO, client-side for dashboard interactivity), Node.js/Python API, PostgreSQL with row-level security. Multi-tenancy: shared database with tenant_id on every table, middleware extracts tenant from subdomain or JWT. RBAC: roles table (admin, editor, viewer), permissions table, role_permissions junction. Check permissions in API middleware and conditionally render UI elements. Real-time dashboards: WebSocket connection per user, server pushes data updates, charts re-render with new data. Data layer: time-series data in TimescaleDB or aggregated in PostgreSQL, Redis for caching computed metrics. White-labeling: tenant config stores logo, colors, custom domain; CSS custom properties for theming, dynamic favicon/title. Frontend: chart library (Recharts/Chart.js), dashboard builder with drag-and-drop widgets, saved layouts per user. Performance: pre-compute aggregations, cache dashboard data with short TTL, lazy-load chart libraries. Security: tenant isolation enforced at database level (RLS policies), API rate limiting per tenant, audit logging.
follow_ups: How do you handle custom domains for white-labeled tenants?
How do you ensure one tenant's heavy queries don't affect others?
How would you implement a dashboard builder with drag-and-drop widgets?

questions.question: Design a marketplace platform like Airbnb — search, booking, payments, buyer-seller messaging. How would you structure that entire system?
questions.expected_answer: Services: API gateway → user service, listing service, search service, booking service, payment service, messaging service. Search: Elasticsearch for full-text + geo search, faceted filtering, relevance scoring. Sync listing data from PostgreSQL to ES via CDC or event stream. Booking: state machine (available → pending → confirmed → completed/cancelled), pessimistic locking on availability to prevent double-booking, calendar management. Payments: Stripe Connect for marketplace (hold payment → release on completion), escrow pattern, refund handling. Messaging: WebSocket for real-time, persist messages in database, push notifications for offline users. Frontend: Next.js with SSR for listing pages (SEO), client-side for dashboard/messaging. Map integration (Mapbox/Google Maps) for geo-based search. Image handling: S3 + CloudFront CDN, multiple sizes generated on upload. Trust & Safety: review system, identity verification, content moderation. Mobile: responsive web + potentially React Native for native app.
follow_ups: How do you prevent double-booking for popular listings?
How do you handle payment disputes and refunds?
How would you implement smart pricing suggestions for sellers?

questions.question: You're building a platform with both a web app and a public API — rate limiting, versioning, webhooks, developer docs. How do you share a codebase and handle all this?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: shared core business logic layer, with separate presentation layers for web app and public API. API gateway handles routing, auth (session-based for web, API key + OAuth for public API), rate limiting (tiered by plan). API versioning: URL-based (/v1/, /v2/) with version-specific request/response transformers wrapping shared core logic. Webhook system: event-driven — business events published to message queue → webhook dispatcher service → HTTP POST to registered URLs with retry (exponential backoff, 3 attempts), signature verification (HMAC), delivery logs. Rate limiting: token bucket per API key, different limits per endpoint and plan tier, Redis-backed distributed counter. Developer portal: auto-generated API docs from OpenAPI spec, interactive API explorer (like Swagger UI), SDK generation (openapi-generator for Python/JS/Go), webhook testing tool. Monitoring: per-customer usage dashboards, API latency percentiles, error rate by endpoint/version, webhook delivery success rate. Migration strategy: deprecation timeline, sunset headers, usage analytics to identify migration targets.
follow_ups: How do you handle breaking changes in the public API without disrupting existing integrations?
How do you design the webhook system to handle unreliable consumer endpoints?
What's your approach to API key management and rotation?

questions.question: You have a monolithic app serving 10M monthly active users and need to break it into services. How would you approach that migration?
questions.expected_answer: Phase 1 — Understand: map the monolith's domain boundaries (user management, billing, content, notifications, analytics), identify the highest-pain coupling points and the most independently-deployable domains. Phase 2 — Strangler fig pattern: don't rewrite, incrementally extract. Start with the least coupled, highest-churn domain. Put an API gateway in front of the monolith, route specific paths to new services. Phase 3 — Data decomposition: the hardest part. Each service owns its data. Extract tables into separate databases, replace direct joins with API calls or event-driven synchronization. Use CDC (Debezium) during migration to keep data in sync. Phase 4 — Shared infrastructure: event bus (Kafka) for async communication, distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), centralized logging, shared auth service (JWT validation). Phase 5 — Frontend: initially, one frontend talks to gateway. Over time, consider micro-frontends if teams need independent deployment. Key risks: distributed transactions (use saga pattern), increased operational complexity, network latency between services. Success metrics: deployment frequency per service, mean time to recovery, team autonomy (can deploy without coordinating).
follow_ups: How do you handle distributed transactions across services?
What's your approach to testing in a service-oriented architecture?
How do you decide the right granularity for service boundaries?

questions.question: ETL versus ELT — what's the difference, and when would you pick one over the other?
questions.expected_answer: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load): data is extracted from sources, transformed in a staging area (clean, join, aggregate), then loaded into the target system. Good when: target system has limited compute (traditional data warehouse), transformations are complex and need dedicated processing, data quality must be enforced before loading. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform): data is extracted and loaded raw into the target system, then transformed in place using the target's compute. Good when: target is a modern cloud data warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) with massive compute, you want to preserve raw data for flexibility, transformations can be expressed in SQL. Modern trend: ELT is preferred because cloud warehouses are cheap and powerful, raw data preservation enables re-processing, and dbt makes SQL transformations manageable. Consider: data sensitivity (PII should be transformed before loading in some cases), cost (cloud warehouse compute costs for ELT), complexity of transformations.
follow_ups: What tools would you use for each approach?
How does dbt fit into the ELT pattern?
When would you still prefer ETL in a modern stack?

questions.question: Design a data pipeline for user behavior events — collecting them from a web app, storing them, analyzing them. Walk me through it.
questions.expected_answer: Collection: JavaScript SDK on frontend captures events (page views, clicks, form submissions) with metadata (timestamp, user_id, session_id, page URL, device info). Send events to backend endpoint via HTTP POST, batch events to reduce requests. Ingestion: backend API receives events → validates schema → publishes to message queue (Kafka or SQS). Storage: raw events land in data lake (S3 as Parquet files, partitioned by date). Processing: daily batch job (Spark or SQL in warehouse) aggregates events into analytics tables — daily active users, funnel conversion rates, feature usage counts. Warehouse: load aggregated data into Snowflake/BigQuery. Analytics: connect BI tool (Metabase, Looker) to warehouse for dashboards and ad-hoc queries. Data quality: validate event schema on ingestion, monitor event volume for anomalies, check for duplicate events (dedup by event_id). Consider: user consent/privacy (GDPR), event schema versioning, handling late-arriving events.
follow_ups: How do you handle schema evolution as new event types are added?
How do you ensure events aren't lost during ingestion?
How would you build a real-time dashboard from this data?

questions.question: Design a data warehouse for a company pulling data from everywhere — product database, CRM, payments, marketing tools. You need to make it all available for analytics. How do you do it?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture: medallion architecture (bronze → silver → gold). Bronze: raw data ingested from sources, stored as-is in data lake (S3 Parquet). Sources: PostgreSQL via CDC (Debezium), Salesforce CRM via API sync, Stripe webhooks, Google Analytics/marketing APIs. Silver: cleaned, deduplicated, standardized data — resolve schema differences, handle nulls, normalize timestamps to UTC, apply business rules. Gold: analytics-ready tables — star schema with fact tables (orders, events, interactions) and dimension tables (users, products, campaigns). Tools: ingestion (Fivetran/Airbyte for managed connectors, custom for complex sources), transformation (dbt for SQL-based transforms with testing and documentation), orchestration (Airflow for scheduling and dependency management). Warehouse: Snowflake or BigQuery. Key patterns: slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 2 for historical tracking), incremental processing (process only new/changed data), data quality tests at each layer. Governance: data catalog (dbt docs or DataHub), column-level lineage, access controls per dataset, PII classification and masking.
follow_ups: How do you handle slowly changing dimensions?
What's your approach to data quality testing across layers?
How do you manage access control to sensitive data?

questions.question: Design the analytics system for an A/B testing platform — tracking results, computing significance, giving product teams self-service reports. How do you build that?
questions.expected_answer: Data collection: events tagged with experiment_id and variant (control/treatment), collected via event pipeline into warehouse. Metrics computation: daily batch job calculates per-user metrics for each experiment (conversion rate, revenue, engagement). Pre-compute metric aggregates per variant per day. Statistical analysis: for each experiment, compute test statistic (z-test for proportions, t-test for continuous metrics), p-value, confidence interval, effect size. Sequential testing (always-valid p-values) for teams that want to peek early. Sample size calculator: input expected effect size, baseline rate, significance level → output required sample per variant. Reporting: self-service dashboard per experiment showing variant comparison, metric trends over time, statistical significance indicator, guardrail metrics. Architecture: event pipeline → warehouse → dbt models for metric computation → statistical analysis service (Python/R) → dashboard (Metabase/Looker). Governance: minimum experiment duration before significance check, multiple comparison correction when testing multiple metrics, sample ratio mismatch detection as data quality check.
follow_ups: How do you handle the multiple comparison problem when testing many metrics?
What's sample ratio mismatch and why is it important?
How would you handle long-running experiments where novelty effects are a concern?

questions.question: You're building data processing for a fintech company — you need real-time fraud detection and batch regulatory reporting. How do you design that dual architecture?
questions.expected_answer: Dual architecture: Lambda or Kappa pattern. Real-time path: transaction events → Kafka → Flink/Spark Streaming for fraud detection (rule engine + ML model scoring), sub-second latency requirement. Enrichment: join with user profile and transaction history from feature store. Decisions: approve/flag/block in real-time, push results to API and alert system. Batch path: same raw events from Kafka → S3 (data lake) → daily Spark jobs for regulatory reports (transaction summaries, suspicious activity reports, compliance metrics). Reconciliation: compare real-time decisions with batch re-processing to catch discrepancies. Data quality: schema registry (Avro/Protobuf) for event contracts, data validation at ingestion, automated data quality checks (Great Expectations). Governance: full audit trail (every decision traceable to input data), data retention policies per regulation, encryption at rest and in transit, access logging. Infrastructure: Kafka for event backbone, Flink for stream processing, Spark for batch, S3 for storage, Snowflake for analytics. Monitoring: processing lag, event throughput, error rates, fraud detection precision/recall.
follow_ups: How do you ensure consistency between real-time and batch results?
How do you handle late-arriving events in regulatory reports?
What's your approach to data lineage for audit compliance?

questions.question: Design a data platform that serves both data engineering and data science teams — shared assets, governance, self-service access. How would you structure it?
questions.expected_answer: Platform layers: 1) Ingestion — managed connectors (Fivetran/Airbyte) for SaaS sources, CDC for databases, custom pipelines for APIs. 2) Storage — data lake (S3) organized by medallion layers, cloud warehouse (Snowflake) for analytics. 3) Processing — dbt for SQL transforms, Spark for heavy processing, Airflow for orchestration. 4) Data science — notebook environments (JupyterHub/SageMaker), access to warehouse and lake data, compute isolation (separate warehouse for DS queries). 5) Governance — data catalog (DataHub/Atlan) with automated metadata, column-level lineage, PII classification with automated tagging, access requests and approval workflow. Self-service: curated gold-layer datasets with documentation, SQL query interface, business metrics definitions in semantic layer (dbt metrics or Cube). Quality: automated tests in dbt, anomaly detection on key metrics, data freshness SLAs per dataset. Cost management: query cost attribution per team, storage tiering (hot/cold), idle resource monitoring. Security: role-based access at dataset level, dynamic data masking for PII, audit logging for compliance.
follow_ups: How do you balance data democratization with governance and security?
How do you handle schema evolution without breaking downstream consumers?
What's your approach to data quality SLAs?

questions.question: You're scaling a data infrastructure from 10GB to 10TB daily, with 50+ consumers across analytics, ML, and product teams. How do you architect that?
questions.expected_answer: Architecture evolution: move from single-warehouse to data mesh-inspired architecture. Domain teams own their data products (well-defined datasets with SLAs, documentation, quality guarantees). Central platform provides self-service infrastructure. Infrastructure: event backbone (Kafka with schema registry for cross-domain events), data lake (S3 with Iceberg/Delta Lake for ACID transactions and time-travel), cloud warehouse (Snowflake with multi-cluster scaling). Processing: real-time (Flink for streaming enrichment), batch (Spark/dbt for transformations), micro-batch for near-real-time analytics. Data products: each domain publishes curated datasets with: schema contract, freshness SLA, quality score, documentation, owner. Consumers discover via data catalog. Cost management: chargeback model — compute and storage costs attributed to producing and consuming teams. Storage tiering: hot (warehouse), warm (lake), cold (glacier). Query optimization: materialized views for common patterns, query governor for expensive scans. Governance: federated — central policies (PII handling, retention, access framework), domain-level implementation. Lineage: end-to-end from source to dashboard, automated impact analysis for schema changes. Reliability: pipeline SLOs, automated alerting, incident response playbook for data outages.
follow_ups: How do you implement a data mesh without creating data silos?
What's your approach to cost optimization when data volume grows 10x?
How do you handle cross-domain data quality when each domain owns their data?

questions.question: You're building data governance and compliance across multiple regions with strict privacy reqs like GDPR and CCPA. How do you design that framework?
questions.expected_answer: Framework layers: 1) Classification — automated PII detection and tagging (names, emails, IPs, etc.), data sensitivity levels (public, internal, confidential, restricted), classification stored in data catalog metadata. 2) Access control — attribute-based access control (ABAC) considering role, data sensitivity, purpose, and geography. Dynamic data masking for PII (analysts see masked data, specific roles see raw). 3) Residency — geo-aware data routing, EU personal data stays in EU region, cross-region analytics via aggregated/anonymized datasets. 4) Consent management — consent database tracking per-user permissions, data processing filtered by consent status, right-to-be-forgotten implementation (soft delete + hard delete pipeline). 5) Retention — automated retention policies per data category, deletion workflows with verification, legal hold capability for litigation. 6) Audit — comprehensive access logging, query logging, data lineage for impact analysis, regular compliance reports. Technical implementation: policy engine evaluates access requests against rules, row-level security in warehouse, encryption (at rest with KMS, in transit with TLS, field-level for sensitive columns). Organizational: Data Protection Officer, privacy engineering team, privacy impact assessments for new data use cases, regular training for data practitioners.
follow_ups: How do you implement right-to-be-forgotten across a distributed data ecosystem?
How do you enable cross-region analytics while complying with data residency requirements?
What's your approach to automated PII detection in unstructured data?
